title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] SINEAD BOVELL: Hi, everyone. My name is Sinead Bovell. I am a futurist and
strategic foresight advisor, and I am really excited to
introduce somebody that would be considered a celebrity to me. So he is a titan of computing. He has shaped the
very foundations of modern technology. His work has shaped
billions of devices and transformed
our digital world, and to not miss any
of his accolades, at UC Berkeley, Pardee
emeritus, and a Google distinguished engineer. He literally wrote the book
on computer architecture. And not just one of them,
he wrote seven of them. His unparalleled
contributions have earned him the Nobel prizes of computing
and engineering, the Turing Award, and the Draper prize. Please join me in giving a huge
welcome to Dr. David Patterson. [APPLAUSE] DAVID PATTERSON: All right. SINEAD BOVELL: Thank you. DAVID PATTERSON: Well,
thanks for sticking around at to the end. I hope the best is last. You'll have to get your vote. So this was a
controversial title. People in comms weren't thrilled
with the "How to Give AI a Bad Carbon Footprint." But I was a Berkeley
professor for four decades before I joined Google,
and in that time, I gave a series of
bad advice talks. And my friend and I
thought to teach students how to give a talk, we'd tell
them how to give a bad talk. And then if you don't want to do
that, you do it the other way. So we gave a set of
commandments that if you follow, you're guaranteed
to be a bad talk. And then I did one on how to
have a bad career and then how to do a bad research lab. And this is my fourth one
that we're debuting today. So the first part is how
to do it badly and then, if you don't want to do that,
how to avoid doing it badly. And then part of
my textbooks that I wrote with John Hennessy,
my co-author on all those, that we have a fallacy section
at the end of every chapter. This sounds right, but
it doesn't quite work. So I'll go over some fallacies. And there's a lot
of fallacies when we get to talking
about carbon footprint.

text:
that we have a fallacy section
at the end of every chapter. This sounds right, but
it doesn't quite work. So I'll go over some fallacies. And there's a lot
of fallacies when we get to talking
about carbon footprint. And then Sinead and I
will do a little bit of question and answer. So let's do the
how to do it badly, how to give it a bad
carbon footprint. So the advice would be
pick the biggest model, and bigger is better. We know from the
scaling laws of OpenAI that the bigger it is, the
smarter it is, so go big. Pick an older GPU. The more recent GPUs, like are
1000 watts when they operate, the older ones could
be one third of that, so let's go with an older one. And just pick our
local data center. We don't want to have to
ship the data all over. We'll just go locally. So let's see how that turns out. So to calculate the
emissions we came up with a mnemonic we call
the four Ms of emissions. And each of these is a factor. When you multiply them together,
you get the emissions from AI. So for a model, we're going to
pick this open source model, the Llama model from Meta. It's the 405 billion
parameter model. So the first thing
we're going to count is the number of operations and,
in computer terms, the number of floating point operations. So that turns out to be
that number of 3.8 times 10 to the 25th. 10 to the 24 is a septillion. That might be what
you learned today. So about 40 septillion
floating point operations to train that llama model, so
that's the number of operations. Next, depending on
the hardware we pick, it'll turn those
operations into energy. So the Volta, which
was an older GPU, was the first one with
hardware to support AI hardware acceleration. So it operates at a third of-- about 300 watts and
about 25 teraflops. So that turns out you get
an energy for floating point operations out of that. So if you multiply
those two together, we get how much energy? But it has to go
in a data center. So then the question is, how
efficient is the data center? It turns out that
the industry has
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
operations out of that. So if you multiply
those two together, we get how much energy? But it has to go
in a data center. So then the question is, how
efficient is the data center? It turns out that
the industry has standardized on a metric
that's called power usage effectiveness. And you just take all the energy
going into the data center, and you divide it by the energy
that the computers actually use. So some local data centers will
have a power usage effectiveness of 2, which means about
only half of the energy goes to equipment,
and half of it goes for
power-distributed cooling. So that's pretty inefficient. So when you multiply the
model times the machine times what we call mechanization
to get an M out of it, that's how much energy you use. And then I think one of the big
surprises when we got into this was how variable
it is by geography, that the energy intensity or the
carbon intensity varies a lot. So to make it bad, we're
going to pick West Virginia because there's a
lot of coal mines there, so the public
policy is to create energy by burning coal. So if you look at the
bottom of the slide, we put those four
factors together, and you end up with this
number 2.4 times 10 to the 5th kilograms. In this community, the numbers
are so big they use tons. That's British tons. That's 1000 kilograms. Americans call it metric tons. So 240,000 metric tons, that's
the emissions for training the Llama model. That's what we're trying to do. That's the bad example. So if we don't want it
to be bad, what do we do? Well, don't do the
ones on the right. Do the ones on the left. Instead of picking
the biggest model, pick the most efficient model. That makes sense. And it turns out you don't
want to use an older GPU, even if it uses less energy
while it's operating. You want to use the
latest GPU or TPU because it's so much faster. So it uses more
energy per second, but it takes fewer
seconds to run. And then you have to worry
about the mechanization. How efficient is data center?

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE

You want to use the
latest GPU or TPU because it's so much faster. So it uses more
energy per second, but it takes fewer
seconds to run. And then you have to worry
about the mechanization. How efficient is data center? Well, the cloud companies have
been building giant data centers for 20 years, so they're
really good at making them very efficient. So we'll see what the
numbers turn out to be. And then since we're in the
cloud, we can train it anywhere. Let's not train it
in West Virginia that burns coal for energy. Let's train it someplace else
that's much more efficient. OK, so let's go to
our four Ms again. Instead of the Llama model,
we're going to use Google's open source model, Gemma 3 27B. That's about the same
quality as the Llama model that Google has done. How many floating
point operations? It's about two septillion
floating point operations. That's almost a factor of 20
better than the lambda model. So we get a factor of 20 there. Let's go to the machine. Not surprisingly, I'm going
to use the latest Google TPU. That's about a factor of 4
better than the old NVIDIA GPU. And I told you when we used
the cloud data centers, the power usage
effectiveness is really good. It's only about 10% tax. So 90% of all the energy is
going through the equipment, and about 10% goes for power
distribution and cooling. And then finally,
like I said, maps, given we got to turn all that
energy into emissions, let's go to the place that's got the
lowest emissions per megawatt hour. And that, if we keep it in a
place with hydroelectric power, like the Northwest, then
we get a much lower number. The last slide, it was 240,000. This slide is 150 tons. And if we go
through the numbers, there was that factor of 20
for model times a factor of 4. That's a factor of 80. And then the mechanization
is about 2, so that's 160. And maps is about
a factor of 10. That's 1,600. And that's very close. So we could make
it bad 1,500 times, 1,600 times worse than it
was than if we make it good. So you saw the
wrong way to do it,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
And then the mechanization
is about 2, so that's 160. And maps is about
a factor of 10. That's 1,600. And that's very close. So we could make
it bad 1,500 times, 1,600 times worse than it
was than if we make it good. So you saw the
wrong way to do it, and now you can see
the right way to do it. Now we're going to move
into the fallacies. And when I got started on
this a couple of years ago, one of the Googlers gave me a
piece of advice going into it. As an engineer, you think
kind of, everything's science. And she said, energy is physics,
so that's still science. But emissions is accounting. So why is it an accounting
rather than a science? Because you have this
long supply chain, and you've got to decide,
well, who gets credit for what? And who is responsible
for what things? So you have to set up
an accounting system to be able to keep it fair and
make it work for everybody. So let's go over the fallacies. So the first one
was that assumption we made that newer hardware is
worse than the older hardware. So I and four or five friends
here at Google just published kind of a breakthrough paper,
and it's called a "Life Cycle Analysis of AI Accelerators"
that had never been done before. So life cycle analysis means you
take it from cradle to grave. So you get the materials from-- mine the materials. And then you put it in
simulated processing. You get chips out of it
and put chips on boards. And you stick them in racks. You have to transport all
that to a Google data center and install it. Then it has its
operational lifetime, so that's the manufacturing
and transportation costs. That's called embodied
carbon footprint. Then there's the operational
piece over its lifetime, say, six years. And then at the end,
you have to recycle it, so we did the whole
cradle-to-grave approach and did the cost. Now, we did this for five TPUs. So the five TPUs break into two
sets of what Google Cloud refers to as the versatile TPUs. That's TPU v4, v5e, and 6e. The 6e's the one
called the Trillium.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
and did the cost. Now, we did this for five TPUs. So the five TPUs break into two
sets of what Google Cloud refers to as the versatile TPUs. That's TPU v4, v5e, and 6e. The 6e's the one
called the Trillium. And so you can see, the carbon
emissions, smaller is better. So it's about a factor
of 3 improvement between those generations,
which just took two years. That's the overall. If we look at finer detail,
the light blue at the bottom, that's the embodied piece. And again, it's
about a factor of 3. If we look over at
the powerful TPUs, this is the ones
that do the training. This is like 4,000 chips in a
pod or 8,000 chips in a pod. It's about a factor
of 1.2 or 1.3, both overall and then
for the [? embodied. ?] Now, I have to get into a little
bit the accounting issues here. So why is that tall bar in
a dark piece and a light a medium blue piece there? Well, that's because what's
called the Greenhouse Gas Protocol has agreed on how we
can count account for emissions. The first one at the top is
called location base, that is, just based on what you
get from the local utility how much emissions do you get. But we want people to buy
clean energy in addition to what you get
from the utility. And so they created
a market for that, so that's called market based. So the light blue bar
there is the market based, and the tall one is
the location based. But no matter how
you account for it, it's still this dramatic
reduction for the versatile TPUs and a significant reduction
for the powerful TPUs. So the fallacy was
[INAUDIBLE] was worse. That was a long explanation
of how it's actually better. So that was fortunate. We didn't know what was
going to happen if-- it would have been sad for our
leadership if we did the study and said, hey, don't
use the new TPUs. Use the old TPUs. But it turned out
to be the best. Basically, they
use more emissions, but they're so much faster,
They're a better thing to use. This one's going to take
a little longer to do. You see, I've got a
movie set facade here,

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE

But it turned out
to be the best. Basically, they
use more emissions, but they're so much faster,
They're a better thing to use. This one's going to take
a little longer to do. You see, I've got a
movie set facade here, so that's kind of a hint of
what the story is going to be. It looks good from
the front, but if you go in the back of
the movie set, you see it's just an
empty space and iron. So I got to tell you
an allegorical story to set the stage, and then I
think it'll be easy to relate. So the allegory is you and your
friend Max, you're best friends. You're single. You're young. And you hate to cook, so
you eat all your meals out at restaurants, three meals
a day, 365 days a year. You start getting worried about
your health for both of you but particularly
yourself so think we should start eating
more organic food. Well, then you find
out there's a society that's trying to promote
organic food in restaurants. And so they put little
stickers on the menu items that are organic. And when you buy organic meals,
you get an organic certificate. And the society to encourage
people to eat more organically spreads towards the
restaurants, and they run a leaderboard where the
winner at the end of the year gets prizes on that. So you get involved. Your friend Max isn't
all that excited. And you get, in
a year, 365 days. There's about 1,100
meals that you'd eat. You get 800, and you turn it in. And then they announce
the prize, and you win. You get accolades. People like you, the
article, newspaper. You feel really good about it. Next year you want
to do it again. So you get up to 900, and
you're waiting for the results. And at 900, you come
in second place. Somebody has 1,000 meals,
even better than you. But the amazing thing is the
person with 1,000 meals is Max. You've gone to meals
with Max all year. He almost never orders organic. So you confront him,
what's going on? So Max says, well, I read
the fine print of the rules. And you don't have to
turn in the receipts for the meals you eat.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
You've gone to meals
with Max all year. He almost never orders organic. So you confront him,
what's going on? So Max says, well, I read
the fine print of the rules. And you don't have to
turn in the receipts for the meals you eat. You just have to turn in
receipts that you buy somewhere. So I looked, and in San
Diego, I could for-- the cheapest places San
Diego in April for breakfast. So I bought 1,000 breakfasts in
San Diego and then turned those receipts in, those organic
receipts, during the year. And that's how I won. Well, then you say to Max, but
A, it's not helping your health. You're not eating
anything organically. Also you're not encouraging
local restaurants to offer organic food because
you're buying in San Diego. And even in San
Diego, you're only buying breakfast,
not the other meals, and you're only
doing it in April. So what do you
think you're doing? Well, I'm getting all the good
press, and I feel good about it. And I like eating the way I am. So that's the long
allegorical story setup. What's the relationship here? So when they set up the
Greenhouse Gas Protocol, this accounting
system for companies to follow, about 20 years
ago, as you might expect, when people were buying
carbon-free energy, they got certificates. They're called energy
attribute certificates. So basically, when you buy clean
energy, for every megawatt, you've got a certificate. But what some people started
doing was kind of Max. Well, I don't want to buy clean
energy from the local one. I'll just buy it in some
other part of the country, and I'll just buy it some
other part of the year. And then I'll take
credit for it. So the difference between those
things, when you buy an energy certificate along with
the energy, that's called a bundled energy
attribute certificate. And when you buy it from
anywhere in the country, then you get an unbundled one. And in fact, the rules
are any time of the year, anywhere in the same continent. So it doesn't even have to
be in the United States.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
And when you buy it from
anywhere in the country, then you get an unbundled one. And in fact, the rules
are any time of the year, anywhere in the same continent. So it doesn't even have to
be in the United States. And in Europe, it can be
any country in Europe. You can be in France. You can buy it in Poland. Well, like your skepticism
about Max benefits of eating organically, academics
have studied the benefit. What happens with
these unbundled EACs. And what they find
is if companies buy unbundled EACs, that doesn't
encourage the local utility to invest in carbon-free
energy because you're buying it someplace else. And similarly, you're
probably buying it as cheap as possible to a place
that already makes it, so even if you're
buying it there, it probably doesn't get
them to invest a lot more. They've already
made the investment. So what they find is the
use of the unbundled EACs doesn't really help that much. So there's a
market-based economy, and it makes people look good. But it doesn't really
help the bottom line. So now you know what unbundled
EACs are and bundles of EACs. So how popular are they? That was the next
part of my journey. And this article appeared in
"The Financial Times" last year. and here's the top for
US hyperscaler companies and what fraction of the
carbon-free energy they bought is unbundled. And you can see
Amazon's almost 60%. Microsoft's more than half. Facebook is very small. And we're 0, which I
was very happy to learn. There's the first column is
the location-based submissions. That's right out of the
tap from the utility. And then if you do
market-based submissions, you can see, for
Microsoft, it looks like 0. They could claim, using
the unbundled EACs, they have zero-carbon footprint. So that was what
the original fallacy that I started this talk with,
that basically I read online that these companies are
making their operational carbon footprint go to 0. So it's just going to
be the manufacturing costs is everything. But no. And the reason is
that it's not really--
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
that I started this talk with,
that basically I read online that these companies are
making their operational carbon footprint go to 0. So it's just going to
be the manufacturing costs is everything. But no. And the reason is
that it's not really-- it looks good on paper,
but it's not really helping the environment
all that much. Now, the good news is
since about the time we wrote this paper and
pointed this out, Microsoft has since announced
in the future they're abandoning unbundled EACs. So why did Google not do that? Why did Google not
buy unbundled EACs? It's because several
years ago they realized this was not
really going to help, and so they came up with
their own accounting system. And what they wanted people to
do, kind of like when you were eating organic food, we wanted
that, for 24 hours a day, you're using carbon-free
energy, 365 days a year and not you buy it in San Diego. You'd buy it where
you're using it. So they call their program
24/7 Carbon-Free Energy. And 160 organizations
have embraced it so far. There's a United Nations
agreement around that. That's my second fallacy. The Third one, which is easier, and
you hear this one every day. When I was listening to
the radio NPR on Saturday, I heard this one. Data Center AI is a major
consumer of global electricity. It's repeated so
much, it must be true. So my data source is the
International Energy Agency, IEA. It's about organization
of 60 companies that have been doing this
together for 50 years, and they're trying to
wean these countries off of carbon-based fuels. So they said in 2022
it was about 1.2% of all electricity
was data centers, and maybe this year
it will be 1.5%. How does that compare
to other things? And in their report, it says
that, even if data center growth is strong between
now and 2030, there's four things that
will be much bigger. Just continued economic
growth will be much bigger. Electric cars will
be much bigger, which I guess you might
guess, given the popularity of electric cars.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
is strong between
now and 2030, there's four things that
will be much bigger. Just continued economic
growth will be much bigger. Electric cars will
be much bigger, which I guess you might
guess, given the popularity of electric cars. But air conditioners
are going to be bigger. Just air conditioners
all by themselves will be a bigger use of
electricity than data centers. And then the last category
was high-energy manufacturing, like making aluminum or steel. So that's the big picture
globally about data centers. What about AI? AI is a small piece
of data centers. So the assertion that
AI is a huge impact on global electricity
is far from the truth. But as a caveat, when we
talk about a global problem, it's not. But maybe locally, it can be. If you put a lot of data
centers in one utility, they may not have the power
to be able to handle that, and that'd be very
stressful for a local area. But as a global issue,
it's not a big issue. Now, when I think about
air conditioners using more than data centers, what are
the upsides of air conditioners? And what are they going
to contribute to society? Well, AI can make
a contribution. So my favorite one is the
science result from Google in the last couple of
years, is contrails. So contrails are
the light streams in the sky that
follow airplanes. It doesn't happen all the time. It happens when airplanes
are in human regions. So aviation represents about
2.5% of worldwide emissions, according to IEA. Contrails, you saw in the
pie chart, more than a third of emissions is from contrails. Well, if it's only occurs when
planes fly in certain regions, well, Google can use
AI to steer planes as minimally as possible in
both an elevation and direction to avoid the human regions,
and they did this experiment with American Airlines. And they found they have to
increase the fuel that you use a little bit, 0.3%, but
it could save more than half of the contrails. So if you multiply that all out,
if all aviation used this idea, it could save 180
million tons of CO2e.
speaker: unknown

text:
And they found they have to
increase the fuel that you use a little bit, 0.3%, but
it could save more than half of the contrails. So if you multiply that all out,
if all aviation used this idea, it could save 180
million tons of CO2e. So to put that in perspective,
the four hyperscalers collectively using the
conservative location-based emissions was 36 million tons. And if we were to guess what
it would be in reality, giving credit for carbon-free
energy, it's probably another factor of 2. So this single innovation from
AI, if it was widely deployed, would give a factor
of 5 or 10 return on the reduction of carbon
emissions for the planet. And I think with that
is my last slide-- oh, no, my conclusions. So what are the takeaways? Hopefully optimizing
those four Ms of machines. We already have
data centers that are very efficient
and mechanization. Keep building better hardware. People like me do that. AI people building
more efficient models, and then make sure
we do it in places that have very clean energy. Companies, beyond using clean
energy from the utilities, should try and
acquire clean energy on the same 24
hours a day locally, like Google is advocating. But the big picture, I'd
say from my three or four years looking at this, we
have to get the numbers right. Global climate change
is a big problem. But if we don't have
the numbers right, we won't be working
on the right problems. And so recently I wrote a
paper about the impact of AI. And I think the
thing to keep in mind is, this saying is it can be
as big a mistake to ignore the upsides of AI,
like the contrail work, as it is to ignore
the downsides. And with that, I think we
welcome Sinead back on stage. And if you want to read more,
there's a bunch of papers. OK. SINEAD BOVELL: Thank you. [APPLAUSE] That was incredible. That was a fascinating
presentation. And even just your slide
about understanding claims if they're
bundled or unbundled, I think that helps
empower all of us when we see these claims to
be able to dig down and see

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
That was a fascinating
presentation. And even just your slide
about understanding claims if they're
bundled or unbundled, I think that helps
empower all of us when we see these claims to
be able to dig down and see what is actually accurate,
what's actually helpful. So in your research for
this presentation today, what excited and surprised you
the most about Google's work in this space? DAVID PATTERSON: Yeah, I
was thinking about this. I think when I actually did the
work, there were two surprises. The one was geography
matters so much that it's a huge difference what
part of the country you're in. And the second one,
which I was happy that I chose after working at
Berkeley to work at Google, is Google has a pretty
good job at this. They weren't using
unbundled EACs. That was great. Actually their data
centers are very efficient. I think Google is one of the
pioneers in trying to come up with this metric of
power usage effectiveness and driving it down. And if you look at-- I think they've got
five data centers that 90% of the energy they
use is carbon-free energy. So it was a very
pleasant surprise that Google is probably a leader
in carbon footprint and data centers and AI. I think it had to
do because maybe the founders of the company
were more interested in environmental issues. But if I had found out
that we were the worst, that would have been very-- I would have to
rethink where I work. SINEAD BOVELL: And
earlier this year, there was some buzz about a
technological advancement from a company
named DeepSeek out of China, that they
had brought an AI model to the forefront
with low levels, lower resource,
commoditized hardware. And then we started to
hear about Jevons paradox. So the cheaper something
becomes, the more of it we actually end up using. So is Jevons paradox
the right framing for the carbon footprint of AI? Because people were
also throwing that in. Or is it something
else entirely? DAVID PATTERSON: Yeah, so
almost every time I give a talk, somebody brings up
the Jevons paradox.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 5MPxzw15ojE
text:
for the carbon footprint of AI? Because people were
also throwing that in. Or is it something
else entirely? DAVID PATTERSON: Yeah, so
almost every time I give a talk, somebody brings up
the Jevons paradox. So Jevons paradox is
kind of economics, that if you make
something cheaper, well, what economists say, if
it's an elastic product, if you make it cheaper,
people use more of it. There are some things
that aren't elastic, like maybe food production. People don't eat more
food if it gets cheaper. But some things, if you
make it cheaper, get bigger. But when we talk about
reducing the carbon footprint, that's not cheaper. That's making it more efficient. In fact, Google spends extra
money to buy carbon-free energy. If they would just use
the cheapest energy, which would be dirtier-- so it gets brought
up in that context, but it really doesn't apply. And the DeepSeek people, their
breakthrough was training. What they claimed was a
breakthrough in training. But when we talk about
operational emissions, generally it's
probably the inference that's the bigger deal. SINEAD BOVELL: Yeah, yeah,
the inference, for sure. What are you doing differently
in your personal life as a result of everything
you've learned in your research these past couple of years? DAVID PATTERSON: Yeah, well,
there's a couple of things. Google Flight, if you search
for a trip on Google Flight, it tells you exactly
what the carbon emissions are of your flight
and depending on where you sit. And pre-COVID,
people in China would ask me to come out and give
a talk and the first class ticket and all that. And then you go
to Google Flight, and I would use
up as much energy as the average American for one
first-class ticket to Beijing. And so I already probably
didn't want to go on a trip, but now I certainly
fly a lot less. The second one was something
we didn't talk about. If people asked when I started
doing these talks about the data center, they said, well, what
about the edge and phones? Well, the big
surprise in that one

The second one was something
we didn't talk about. If people asked when I started
doing these talks about the data center, they said, well, what
about the edge and phones? Well, the big
surprise in that one was, when we talked
it over, well, the phone actually doesn't
draw any energy from the grid. It's the chargers. Well, then we looked
at the chargers, and the chargers were awful. Because it's convenient
to use wireless Chargers, wireless Chargers
are much less energy efficient than wired chargers. So we got rid of all of
our wireless chargers. And also there's this thing
called vampire power, which means, even though
the phone's dead, you've left the charger in. And it's drawing power
even when you don't use it. And so maybe on average,
people have three phones, wireless chargers
plugged in with no phone on it using up electricity. So we've become
more conscientious about leaving things
plugged in and trying to use the most efficient ones. And when we looked
at the price, there was no correlation between
price and energy efficiency. Once again, fortunately,
Google happened to make the most efficient
wireless, wired charger, which was good in our results. But we couldn't see
any relationship. There needs to be
a consumer report. A thing that would make a
big difference on electricity and emissions in
the world would be people making cell phone
chargers more efficient, which is surprising. It seems like we ought
to be able to do that. But there's no
incentive to do that, as far as I can tell right now. SINEAD BOVELL: I feel like
somebody here is probably capable of that as well. DAVID PATTERSON: Oh yeah. SINEAD BOVELL: So you're
a Turing Award winner. You're an icon in the field. And you've been at
this a long time. What advice would
you have for some of the developers in this room? DAVID PATTERSON: Well,
I have two grown sons, and they make fun
of me for saying I think I give good advice. But recently, usually for
about the last decade, when I give a technical
talk at a university,
